name: 43 · Lossy Frame Stream (transport dispatch proof)
description:
Proves the data-channel transport abstraction (docs/25 §6): the `open` control token carries an opaque transport descriptor, and BOTH executors dispatch their adapter off it — so JetStream is just one impl and a second transport drops in with zero SDK or workflow-model change.

The producer's Data/Out channel `frames` declares `transport: nats-latest` — lossy-latest core NATS (no JetStream, no replay, no back-pressure), the semantic opposite of the durable default. The compiler bakes that tag into the manifest; the SDK stamps it into the channel's `open` descriptor; the producer's executor dispatches the lossy publish adapter; the consumer's executor lifts it back out of the descriptor and dispatches the matching subscribe adapter. The producer (`open_output('frames').write(...)`) and consumer (`for f in stream('frames')`) are byte-for-byte the ordinary data-channel API — neither names a transport.

Start → producer (pace 20 binary frames over `nats-latest`) → consumer (drain via stream()) → End. Lossy means `received` may be < `sent`: a consumer that joins or falls behind silently misses frames — that is the contract, not a defect. Run: `mekhan run 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000430 --start-tokens '{}'` (no inputs).
